title: Ensure Web application firewall is set to ON in IBM Cloud  Internet Services (CIS)

input_row.title: Ensure Web application firewall is set to ON in IBM Cloud  Internet Services (CIS)
input_row.description: The Web Application Firewall (WAF) protects against ISO Layer-7 attacks, which can be some of the most tricky. What is a Web Application Firewall? A WAF helps protect web applications by filtering and monitoring HTTP traffic between a web application and the internet. A WAF is an OSI protocol Layer-7 defense in the OSI model, and it is not designed to defend against all types of attacks. Deploying a WAF in front of a web application is like placing a shield between the web application and the internet. A proxy server protects a client machine’s identity by using an intermediary (for outgoing traffic), but a WAF is a type of reverse-proxy that protects the server from exposure by having the client's traffic pass through the WAF before reaching the server (for incoming traffic). Key benefits of a CIS WAF The IBM Cloud™ Internet Services WAF is an easy way to set up, manage, and customize security rules to protect your web applications from common web threats. See the following list for key features: • Easy setup: The CIS WAF is part of our overall service, which takes just a few minutes to set up. After you redirect your DNS to us, you can switch on the WAF and set up the rules you need. • Detailed reporting See greater detail in the reporting, for example, threats blocked by rule/rule group.
input_row.rationale: A WAF or Web Application Firewall helps protect web applications by filtering and monitoring HTTP traffic between a web application and the Internet. Types of attacks WAF can prevent A WAF typically protects web applications from attacks such as cross-site forgery, cross-site-scripting (XSS), file inclusion, and SQL injection, among others. A WAF usually is part of a suite of tools, which together can create a holistic defense against a range of attack vectors. How a WAF works WAF is a type of reverse-proxy that protects the server from exposure by having the client requests pass through the WAF before reaching the server. It acts as shield placed between the web application and the internet. A WAF operates through a set of rules often called policies. These policies aim to protect against vulnerabilities in the application by filtering out malicious traffic. The value of a WAF comes from the speed and ease with which its policy modifications can be implemented, thereby allowing a faster response to varying attack vectors. For example, during a DDoS attack, rate limiting can be implemented by modifying WAF policies.
input_row.audit: The Web Application Firewall setting can be audited using the following mechanisms IBM Cloud Console 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Click Menu icon --> Resource list 3. Select the Cloud Internet Service Instance 4. Select the domain that needs to be audited from the domains drop down 5. Choose the Overview from the left panel (this should be the default landing page) 6. Under Security section Web Application Firewall should show up as Enabled IBM Cloud CLI ibmcloud cis waf-setting -i Instance-Name Displaying WAF setting of domain 'DNS_DOMAIN_ID' ... OK ID waf Mode On
input_row.remediation: The Web Application Firewall can be enabled using the following mechanisms IBM Cloud Console 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Click Menu icon --> Resource list 3. Select the Cloud Internet Service Instance 4. Select the domain that needs to be audited from the domains drop down 5. Choose the Security section from the left panel. 6. Click on the WAF tab in the Security panel. 7. Switch the toggle to On IBM Cloud CLI ibmcloud cis waf-setting-update waf-enable -i Instance- Name Updating WAF setting of domain 'e476aba943b5d7d29b96135c78aa55c9' ... OK WAF setting was updated successfully. ID waf Mode On
input_row.impact: A WAF is an OSI protocol Layer-7 defense in the OSI model, and it is not designed to defend against all types of attacks. When a WAF is enabled on Cloud Internet Services the TLS traffic is terminated at the proxy, unencrypted, inspected for any attacks, and then re-encrypted and forwarded on to the application. Another thing to consider with WAF is that there may be false positives based on the application patterns and clients may get blocked or challenged. This can be mitigated by tuning WAF.
input_row.default_value: The Default value for the Web Application Firewall is Off in Cloud Internet Services.
input_row.additional_information: The proxy mode for the service must be enabled for the traffic to pass through the Web Application Firewall.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control can be fully verified via IBM Cloud APIs and the IBM Cloud CLI commands as specified in the audit section. The control also references direct command syntax for auditing and remediation, indicating that it can be automated. The properties being audited (Web Application Firewall settings) are standard features of the IBM Cloud Internet Services, confirming further automation capability.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_cis_web_application_firewall_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. Use the IBM Cloud CLI command `ibmcloud cis waf-setting -i Instance-Name` to check if the Web Application Firewall is enabled for the specified Cloud Internet Service instance. 
2. The command retrieves the WAF setting for the specified domain.
3. Expected output would indicate `ID waf Mode On` confirming that the WAF is enabled.
4. Confirm compliance: If the output shows `ID waf Mode Off`, then the WAF is not enabled, resulting in a fail status. If it shows `ID waf Mode On`, then the configuration is compliant.
